title: Dry Heat Cooking Methods
content:
DRY HEAT METHODS

Baking: Cooking foods in an oven using dry heat. Best for breads, cakes, casseroles. Temperature guidelines: Very Slow (250-300°F), Slow (300-350°F), Moderate (350-400°F), Hot (400-450°F), Very Hot (450-550°F).

Roasting: Cooking meats, poultry, and game in an oven with dry heat. Similar to baking but typically at higher temperatures. Use a thermometer inserted into the thickest part, not touching bone.

Broiling: Cooking at very high temperature with radiant heat from above. Food is placed 3 inches from heat source (adjust to 4-6 inches for thicker cuts or browning delicate items). Turn frequently for even cooking. Ventilation is essential.

Pan-Broiling: Cooking in a very hot, slightly oiled pan with frequent turning. Pour off excess fat as it renders.

Grilling: Cooking over direct heat from coals or flames. Suitable for steaks, chops, small fowl (under 2 lbs). Sear first to seal juices, then adjust distance from heat.

Deep-Fat Frying: Cooking in large quantity of hot fat (360-390°F depending on food). Use thermometer or bread cube test: 1-inch cube should brown in 20-60 seconds depending on desired temperature. Do not overfill pan; fat should cover food completely. Allow temperature to recover between batches.

Sauteing: Cooking in small quantity of fat over high heat. Pan and fat must be hot enough to sear food immediately. Food should be at 70°F+, dry, and not crowded in pan.

Stir-Frying/Skillet-Panning: High-heat cooking with constant agitation. Vegetables must be uniformly sliced. Cook tougher vegetables first, then add tender ones. Finish with brief steaming covered.
